sections.heading: Interpretation
sections.title: Definitions of key cybersecurity terms in the Act
sections.summary: This section defines critical terms used throughout the Cybersecurity and Cybercrime Act 2021, including 'computer data', 'critical information infrastructure', 'cybersecurity incident', and 'cybersecurity policy'. These definitions establish the legal framework for understanding data protection and cybercrime within Mauritius' jurisdiction. The definitions apply to all entities subject to the Act, including government bodies, service providers, and individuals.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines 'critical information infrastructure' as systems whose disruption could impact national security or essential services, directly relevant to cross-border data transfers involving critical infrastructure.

requirements.text: In this Act – 'access' means gaining entry to a program or computer data stored in a computer system
'computer data' means any representation of facts, information or concepts in a form suitable for processing in a computer system
'critical information infrastructure' means an asset, facility, system, network or process whose incapacity would have a debilitating impact on essential services
'cybersecurity incident' means use, disclosure, breach, modification, theft, loss, corruption or destruction of information

sections.source_quote: In this Act – 'access' means gaining entry to a program or computer data stored in a computer system; 'computer data' means any representation of facts, information or concepts in a form suitable for processing in a computer system; 'critical information infrastructure' means an asset, facility, system, network or process whose incapacity would have a debilitating impact on essential services; 'cybersecurity incident' means use, disclosure, breach, modification, theft, loss, corruption or destruction of information.
sections.relevance_reason: Core definitions for data protection and cyber incidents

sections.heading: Short title
sections.relevance_reason: Short title - irrelevant

sections.heading: Interpretation
sections.title: Definitions of key cybersecurity terms in the Act
sections.summary: This section defines critical terms used throughout the Cybersecurity and Cybercrime Act 2021, including 'computer data', 'critical information infrastructure', 'cybersecurity incident', and 'cybersecurity policy'. These definitions establish the legal framework for understanding data protection and cybercrime within Mauritius' jurisdiction. The definitions apply to all entities subject to the Act, including government bodies, service providers, and individuals.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines 'critical information infrastructure' as systems whose disruption could impact national security or essential services, directly relevant to cross-border data transfers involving critical infrastructure.

requirements.text: In this Act – 'access' means gaining entry to a program or computer data stored in a computer system
'computer data' means any representation of facts, information or concepts in a form suitable for processing in a computer system
'critical information infrastructure' means an asset, facility, system, network or process whose incapacity would have a debilitating impact on essential services
'cybersecurity incident' means use, disclosure, breach, modification, theft, loss, corruption or destruction of information

sections.source_quote: In this Act – 'access' means gaining entry to a program or computer data stored in a computer system; 'computer data' means any representation of facts, information or concepts in a form suitable for processing in a computer system; 'critical information infrastructure' means an asset, facility, system, network or process whose incapacity would have a debilitating impact on essential services; 'cybersecurity incident' means use, disclosure, breach, modification, theft, loss, corruption or destruction of information.
sections.relevance_reason: Core definitions for data protection and cyber incidents

sections.heading: Establishment of National Cybersecurity Committee
sections.title: Establishment of National Cybersecurity Committee
sections.summary: This section establishes the National Cybersecurity Committee, which includes representatives from key government bodies, financial institutions, and cybersecurity experts. The Committee is mandated to advise on cybersecurity, coordinate responses to cyber threats, and implement critical infrastructure protection frameworks. The Committee's composition ensures diverse expertise in handling data security across sectors, including financial services.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires a multi-stakeholder committee to oversee cybersecurity, including financial sector representatives, which impacts cross-border data transfers involving critical infrastructure.

requirements.text: There is established, for the purposes of this Act, the National Cybersecurity Committee
The Committee shall comprise a Chairperson appointed by the Prime Minister
The Committee shall include a representative of the Bank of Mauritius
The Committee shall include a representative of the Financial Services Commission

sections.source_quote: There is established, for the purposes of this Act, the National Cybersecurity Committee. The Committee shall comprise a Chairperson, to be appointed by the Prime Minister; a representative of the Bank of Mauritius; a representative of the Financial Services Commission.
sections.relevance_reason: Committee structure for cybersecurity coordination

sections.heading: Functions of Committee
sections.title: Functions of National Cybersecurity Committee
sections.summary: This section outlines the Committee's functions, including advising the government on cybersecurity, coordinating cyber threat responses, and establishing cybersecurity best practices for critical infrastructure. The Committee must also facilitate international cooperation on cyber incidents and critical infrastructure protection, which directly affects how data is handled across borders in financial and sensitive sectors.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandates the Committee to coordinate critical infrastructure protection and international cyber incident responses, impacting cross-border data transfers involving financial services.

requirements.text: The Committee shall advise the Government on cybersecurity and cybercrime
The Committee shall implement Government policy relating to cybersecurity and cybercrime
The Committee shall coordinate all matters relating to cybersecurity and cybercrime
The Committee shall coordinate and facilitate the implementation of a critical information infrastructure protection framework

sections.source_quote: The Committee shall advise the Government on cybersecurity and cybercrime; implement Government policy relating to cybersecurity and cybercrime; coordinate all matters relating to cybersecurity and cybercrime; coordinate and facilitate the implementation of a critical information infrastructure protection framework.
sections.relevance_reason: core cybersecurity governance for cross-border data

sections.heading: Meetings of Committee
sections.title: Meetings of National Cybersecurity Committee
sections.summary: This section specifies the Committee's meeting schedule, quorum requirements, and administrative duties. The Committee must meet at least once every two months, with a quorum of seven members. The Secretary to the Committee is responsible for preparing meetings and maintaining minutes, ensuring transparency and accountability in decision-making processes related to cybersecurity.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires regular committee meetings with defined quorum and administrative oversight, ensuring timely coordination of cross-border data security measures.

requirements.text: The Committee shall meet as often as is necessary, but at least once every 2 months
At any meeting of the Committee, 7 members shall constitute a quorum
The Secretary shall prepare and attend every meeting of the Committee

sections.source_quote: The Committee shall meet as often as is necessary, but at least once every 2 months. At any meeting of the Committee, 7 members shall constitute a quorum.
sections.relevance_reason: General governance, quorum rules

sections.heading: Reports by Committee
sections.title: Quarterly committee reports to Minister
sections.summary: The Committee must submit reports to the Minister quarterly or as required. The Minister may refer matters to Cabinet or the police for enquiry if deemed appropriate. These provisions establish reporting obligations and referral mechanisms for committee activities.

requirements.text: The Committee shall submit reports to the Minister on a quarterly basis or whenever required
The Minister may refer to Cabinet any matter referred to him under subsection (1)
The Minister may refer any matter referred to him under subsection (1) to the police for enquiry

sections.source_quote: 6. Reports by Committee (1) The Committee shall submit reports to the Minister on a quarterly basis or whenever required.
sections.relevance_reason: Quarterly reporting to Minister on committee activities

sections.heading: Unauthorised access to computer data
sections.title: Unauthorised computer data access offences
sections.summary: Unauthorized access to computer systems constitutes an offence punishable by fines up to one million rupees and penal servitude up to ten years. Access is unauthorized if the person lacks entitlement or authorization from an entitled person. Exceptions include good faith, consent, and specific statutory powers.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires safeguards to prevent unauthorized access to computer systems, relevant for data security assessments in cross-border contexts.

requirements.text: Any person who gains unauthorized access to any program or data held in a computer system shall commit an offence
A person shall not be liable where they are a person with right to control the operation or use of the computer system and exercises such right in good faith
A person shall not be liable where they have express or implied consent of the person empowered to authorize access
A person shall not be liable where they have reasonable grounds to believe they had consent
A person shall not be liable where they are acting pursuant to measures under Part IV of this Act
A person shall not be liable where they are acting in reliance of any statutory power for obtaining information

sections.source_quote: 7. Unauthorised access to computer data (1) Subject to subsection (2), any person who gains unauthorized access to any program or data held in a computer system shall commit an offence and shall, on conviction, be liable to a fine not exceeding one million rupees and to penal servitude for a term not exceeding 10 years.
sections.relevance_reason: Unauthorised access offences directly address data security and cross-border data transfer compliance

sections.heading: Unauthorised interception of computer service
sections.title: Unauthorised computer service interception offences
sections.summary: Unauthorized interception of computer data or electromagnetic emissions constitutes an offence punishable by fines up to one million rupees and penal servitude up to ten years. If interception impairs system operation or modifies data, penalties increase to up to twenty years. Consent from both sender and recipient and lawful duties may exempt from liability.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires safeguards to prevent unauthorized interception of data, relevant for data security assessments in cross-border contexts.

requirements.text: Any person who, by any technical means, wilfully intercepts or causes to be intercepted without authorization any computer data or electromagnetic emissions carrying computer data shall commit an offence
A person convicted of interception causing impaired system operation or modified data shall be liable to a fine not exceeding one million rupees and penal servitude for a term not exceeding 20 years
A person shall not be liable where they have obtained prior consent of both the person who sent the data and the intended recipient
A person shall not be liable where they are acting in reliance on any statutory power
A person shall not be liable where they are acting in the performance of lawful duties, contractual obligations or discharging legal obligations

sections.source_quote: 8. Unauthorised interception of computer service (1) Subject to subsection (4), any person who, by any technical means, wilfully intercepts or causes to be intercepted without authorisation, any computer data, or electromagnetic emissions carrying computer data, or non-public transmissions to, from or within, a computer system shall commit an offence and shall, on conviction, be liable to a fine not exceeding one million rupees and to penal servitude for a term not exceeding 10 years.
sections.relevance_reason: Core cyber-security offence with penalties

sections.heading: Unauthorised interference
sections.title: Unauthorised computer system interference offences
sections.summary: Intentional unauthorized interference with computer systems constitutes an offence punishable by fines up to one million rupees and penal servitude up to ten years. If interference causes financial loss, threats to national security, reputational damage, physical injury, or system failure, penalties increase to up to twenty years. Interference is unauthorized if the person lacks entitlement or consent.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires safeguards to prevent unauthorized interference with data systems, relevant for data security assessments in cross-border contexts.

requirements.text: Any person who intentionally and without authorization hinders the functioning of a computer system by inputting, transmitting, damaging, deleting, deteriorating, altering or suppressing computer data shall commit an offence
A person shall not be liable where they are acting in reliance on statutory powers for obtaining information
A person shall not be liable where they have consent from a person entitled to authorize interference
A person shall not be liable where interference results in financial loss, threats to national security, reputational damage, physical injury, or system failure

sections.source_quote: 9. Unauthorised interference (1) Any person who, intentionally and without authorisation, hinders the functioning of a computer system by inputting, transmitting, damaging, deleting, deteriorating, altering or suppressing computer data shall commit an offence and shall, on conviction, be liable to a fine not exceeding one million rupees and to penal servitude for a term not exceeding 10 years.
sections.relevance_reason: Cybersecurity offences directly impact data protection and cross-border transfers

sections.heading: Access with intent to commit offences
sections.title: Access with intent to commit offences
sections.summary: Intentional unauthorized access to computer systems with the intent to commit an offence constitutes an offence punishable by fines up to one million rupees and penal servitude up to twenty years. This provision addresses malicious access attempts.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires safeguards to prevent unauthorized access with malicious intent, relevant for data security assessments in cross-border contexts.

requirements.text: Any person who, intentionally and without authorization gains access to any computer program or computer data held in a computer system with intent to commit an offence shall commit an offence

sections.source_quote: 10. Access with intent to commit offences Any person who, intentionally and without authorisation, gains access to any computer program or computer data held in a computer system with intent to commit an offence shall commit an offence and shall, on conviction, be liable to a fine not exceeding one million rupees and to penal servitude for a term not exceeding 20 years.
sections.relevance_reason: Cybersecurity offence with penalties

sections.heading: Unauthorised modification of computer data
sections.title: Unauthorised modification of computer data
sections.summary: Intentional unauthorized modification of computer data constitutes an offence punishable by fines up to one million rupees and penal servitude up to twenty years. If modification impairs system operation, access, or data reliability, penalties increase to up to twenty years. Modification is unauthorized if the person lacks entitlement or consent.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires safeguards to prevent unauthorized data modification, relevant for data security assessments in cross-border contexts.

requirements.text: Any person who, intentionally and without authorization modifies computer data shall commit an offence
A person shall not be liable where they are entitled to determine whether modification should be made
A person shall not be liable where they have consent to modification from a person entitled to authorize
A person shall not be liable where modification impairs system operation, access, or data reliability

sections.source_quote: 11. Unauthorised modification of computer data (1) Subject to subsection (3), any person who, intentionally and without authorisation, modifies computer data shall commit an offence and shall, on conviction, be liable to a fine not exceeding one million rupees and penal servitude for a term not exceeding 20 years.
sections.relevance_reason: Cybersecurity offence with penalties for unauthorized data modification

sections.heading: Unauthorised disclosure of password
sections.title: Unauthorised disclosure of access credentials
sections.summary: This section prohibits intentionally disclosing passwords, access codes, biometric authentication, tokens, or multi-factor authentication without authorization to computer systems or data. Offenders face fines up to one million rupees and penal servitude of up to ten years.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires strict access control measures to prevent unauthorized disclosure of credentials during data transfers or system access.

requirements.text: Must not intentionally disclose passwords, access codes, biometric authentication, tokens, or multi-factor authentication without authorization

sections.source_quote: Any person who, intentionally and without authorisation, discloses any password, access code, biometric authentication, token, two-factor authentication, multi-factor authentication or any other means of gaining access to any computer program or computer data held in any computer system for its production, sale, procurement for use, import or distribution shall commit an offence
sections.relevance_reason: Prohibits unauthorized disclosure of credentials

sections.heading: Unlawful possession of devices and computer data
sections.title: Unlawful possession of malicious computing devices
sections.summary: This section prohibits manufacturing, selling, procuring for use, importing, distributing, or making available of computer systems or data designed for committing offences under this Act. It also prohibits unauthorized possession of such devices or data, with penalties up to one million rupees and ten years penal servitude.
sections.dtia_summary: Addresses security risks from malicious software and devices that could compromise data transfers or systems.

requirements.text: Must not manufacture, sell, procure for use, import, distribute or make available computer systems or data designed for committing offences under this Act
Must not intentionally receive or be in possession of devices and computer data under subsection (1)

sections.source_quote: Any person who intentionally manufactures, sells, procures for use, imports, distributes or otherwise makes available, a computer system, computer data or any other device, designed or adapted primarily for the purpose of committing any offence under this Act shall commit an offence.
sections.relevance_reason: Prohibits malicious devices/data, directly impacts data security and compliance

sections.heading: Electronic fraud
sections.title: Electronic fraud through data manipulation
sections.summary: This section prohibits intentionally causing loss of property by altering, deleting, suppressing data, or interfering with computer systems without authorization. Offenders face fines up to one million rupees and penal servitude of up to twenty years.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires safeguards against data manipulation during cross-border transfers to prevent financial fraud.

requirements.text: Must not intentionally cause loss of property by altering, deleting or suppressing data
Must not intentionally interfere with computer system functioning to procure advantage

sections.source_quote: Any person who, intentionally and without authorisation, causes loss of property to another person by any input, alteration, deletion or suppression of data; or any interference with the functioning of a computer system, to procure for himself or another person any form of advantage shall commit an offence
sections.relevance_reason: Prohibits data manipulation causing financial loss

sections.heading: Computer-related forgery
sections.title: Computer-related forgery of data
sections.summary: This section prohibits intentionally inputting, altering, deleting, or suppressing computer data to create inauthentic data that is treated as authentic for legal purposes. Offenders face fines up to one million rupees and penal servitude of up to ten years for non-wrongful gain, and up to twenty years for wrongful gain or loss.
sections.dtia_summary: Addresses risks of data forgery in cross-border transfers that could lead to financial or legal fraud.

requirements.text: Must not intentionally input, alter, delete or suppress computer data to create inauthentic data treated as authentic for legal purposes

sections.source_quote: Any person who, intentionally and without authorisation, inputs, alters, deletes, or suppresses computer data, resulting in inauthentic data, with the intent that it be considered or acted upon for legal purposes as if it were authentic, regardless of whether or not the data is directly readable and intelligible, shall commit an offence
sections.relevance_reason: Prohibits data forgery, critical for data integrity and security

sections.heading: Misuse of fake profile
sections.title: Misuse of fake profiles for harm
sections.summary: This section prohibits using fake profiles to cause harm, with penalties including fines up to one million rupees and penal servitude of up to twenty years.
sections.dtia_summary: Relevant for data transfers involving fake profiles that could compromise user data security.

requirements.text: Must not use fake profiles to cause harm

sections.source_quote: Any person who individually, or with other persons, makes use of a fake profile to cause harm shall commit an offence
sections.relevance_reason: Prohibits misuse of fake profiles causing harm

sections.heading: Cyberbullying
sections.title: Cyberbullying as an offence
sections.summary: This section prohibits committing cyberbullying, with penalties including fines up to one million rupees and penal servitude of up to twenty years.
sections.dtia_summary: Relevant for data transfers involving cyberbullying that could impact user data security.

requirements.text: Must not commit cyberbullying

sections.source_quote: Any person who, individually or with other persons, commits cyberbullying, shall commit an offence
sections.relevance_reason: Penalties for cyberbullying not directly related to data compliance

sections.heading: Cyber extortion
sections.title: Cyber extortion offences and penalties
sections.summary: This section defines cyber extortion as an offence where a person intentionally engages in cyber extortion activities. Conviction results in a fine of up to one million rupees and penal servitude of up to 20 years.

requirements.text: Any person who engages in cyber extortion shall commit an offence
On conviction, liable to a fine not exceeding one million rupees
On conviction, liable to penal servitude for a term not exceeding 20 years

sections.source_quote: Cyber extortion: Any person who engages in cyber extortion shall commit an offence and shall, on conviction, be liable to a fine not exceeding one million rupees and to penal servitude for a term not exceeding 20 years.
sections.relevance_reason: Cyber extortion penalties apply to data breaches but not directly to data transfers

sections.heading: Revenge pornography
sections.title: Revenge pornography offences and penalties
sections.summary: This section prohibits the disclosure or publication of sexual photographs or films without consent, with intent to cause distress. Conviction leads to a fine of up to one million rupees and penal servitude of up to 20 years.

requirements.text: Any person who discloses or publishes a sexual photograph or film without consent of the person appearing
With the intention of causing distress
On conviction, liable to a fine not exceeding one million rupees
On conviction, liable to penal servitude for a term not exceeding 20 years

sections.source_quote: Revenge pornography: Any person who, by means of a computer system, discloses or publishes a sexual photograph or film without the consent of the person who appears in the photograph or film, and with the intention of causing that person distress, shall commit an offence and shall, on conviction, be liable to a fine not exceeding one million rupees and to penal servitude for a term not exceeding 20 years.
sections.relevance_reason: Revenge porn not data protection

sections.heading: Cyberterrorism
sections.title: Cyberterrorism offences and definitions
sections.summary: This section criminalizes intentional access to computer systems or networks for terrorism purposes. It defines 'act of terrorism' as per the Prevention of Terrorism Act. Conviction results in a fine of up to one million rupees and penal servitude of up to 20 years.

requirements.text: Any person who intentionally accesses or causes to be accessed a computer system or network for the purpose of carrying out an act of terrorism shall commit an offence
On conviction, liable to a fine not exceeding one million rupees
On conviction, liable to penal servitude for a term not exceeding 20 years
In this section, 'act of terrorism' has the same meaning as in the Prevention of Terrorism Act

sections.source_quote: Cyberterrorism: (1) Any person who intentionally accesses or causes to be accessed a computer system or network for the purpose of carrying out an act of terrorism, shall commit an offence and shall, on conviction, be liable to a fine not exceeding one million rupees and to penal servitude for a term not exceeding 20 years. (2) In this section – 'act of terrorism' has the same meaning as in the Prevention of Terrorism Act.
sections.relevance_reason: Criminalizes terrorism, not data protection

sections.heading: Infringement of copyright and related rights
sections.title: Copyright infringement offences and penalties
sections.summary: This section prohibits unauthorized use, publication, or distribution of copyrighted works via computer systems for commercial purposes. It also covers downloading pirated content for gain or against remuneration. First convictions carry fines up to 300,000 rupees and 2 years imprisonment; subsequent convictions carry fines up to 500,000 rupees and 8 years imprisonment.

requirements.text: Any person who, without express authorisation of the author or owner of copyright, attempts to use, publish or distribute another person's work for commercial purpose through a computer system
Downloads movies, music files or pirated software applications for gain or against remuneration
Posts a copyrighted work such as writing or graphics online for gain or against remuneration
On a first conviction, liable to a fine not exceeding 300,000 rupees and imprisonment for a term not exceeding 2 years
On a second or subsequent conviction, liable to a fine not exceeding 500,000 rupees and imprisonment for a term not exceeding 8 years

sections.source_quote: Infringement of copyright and related rights: (1) Any person who, without the express authorisation of the author or owner of the copyright – (a) attempts to use, publish or distribute another person's work for commercial purpose, through a computer system; (b) downloads movies, music files or pirated software applications for gain or against remuneration; or (c) posts a copyrighted work such as writing or graphics, online for gain or against remuneration, shall commit an offence. (2) Any person convicted under subsection (1) shall, on – (a) a first conviction, be liable to a fine not exceeding 300,000 rupees and to imprisonment for a term not exceeding 2 years; (b) a second or subsequent conviction, be liable to a fine not exceeding 500,000 rupees and to imprisonment for a term not exceeding 8 years.
sections.relevance_reason: Copyright infringement unrelated to data protection

sections.heading: Increased penalty for offences involving critical information
sections.title: Increased penalties for critical information infrastructure offences
sections.summary: This section imposes harsher penalties for offences involving critical information infrastructure, including a fine of up to 2 million rupees and imprisonment of up to 25 years for convictions under sections 7, 8, 9, 10, and 11.

requirements.text: Any person who commits an offence specified in sections 7, 8, 9, 10 and 11 on critical information infrastructure
On conviction, liable to a fine not exceeding 2 million rupees
On conviction, liable to imprisonment for a term not exceeding 25 years

sections.source_quote: Increased penalty for offences involving critical information infrastructure: Any person who commits an offence specified in sections 7, 8, 9, 10 and 11 on a critical information infrastructure shall, on conviction, be liable to a fine not exceeding 2 million rupees and to imprisonment for a term not exceeding 25 years.
sections.relevance_reason: penalties for critical infrastructure breaches

sections.heading: Failure to moderate undesirable content
sections.title: Failure to moderate undesirable content
sections.summary: This section mandates online account administrators to moderate undesirable content reported by investigatory authorities. Failure to do so constitutes an offence with penalties of up to one million rupees and 20 years' penal servitude. Undesirable content includes deceptive, defamatory, threatening, or racist material.

requirements.text: Administrator of an online account must moderate and control undesirable content brought to attention by an investigatory authority
Any person who contravenes subsection (1) shall commit an offence
On conviction, liable to a fine not exceeding one million rupees
On conviction, liable to penal servitude for a term not exceeding 20 years
Undesirable content includes online content that is deceptive or inaccurate, posted with intent to defame, threaten, abuse or mislead the public
Undesirable content includes online content that threatens public health or public safety
Undesirable content includes online content that threatens national security
Undesirable content includes online content that promotes racism

sections.source_quote: Failure to moderate undesirable content: (1) It shall be the responsibility of the administrator of an online account to moderate and control undesirable content that has been brought to his attention by an investigatory authority. (2) Any person who contravenes subsection (1) shall commit an offence and shall, on conviction, be liable to a fine not exceeding one million rupees and to penal servitude for a term not exceeding 20 years. (3) For the purpose of this section – 'undesirable content' includes any online content that – (a) is deceptive or inaccurate, posted with intent to defame, threaten, abuse or mislead the public; (b) threatens public health or public safety; (c) threatens national security; or (d) promotes racism.
sections.relevance_reason: Regulator powers and penalties for content moderation

sections.heading: Disclosure of details of an investigation
sections.title: Confidentiality of criminal investigations
sections.summary: This section prohibits disclosure of details of criminal investigations under the Act and mandates confidentiality of such investigations. It imposes a criminal offence for unauthorized disclosure, with penalties including fines up to one million rupees and penal servitude up to 20 years. Exceptions apply where actions are taken under statutory powers, lawful duties, contractual obligations, or legal obligations.
sections.dtia_summary: Confidentiality requirements for criminal investigations impact data handling during investigations but do not directly govern cross-border data transfers.

requirements.text: No person shall disclose any detail of a criminal investigation under this Act
The investigation shall be kept confidential
Any person who contravenes subsection (1) shall commit an offence
On conviction, the contravenor shall be liable to a fine not exceeding one million rupees
On conviction, the contravenor shall be liable to penal servitude for a term not exceeding 20 years
A person shall not be liable under subsection (2) where he is acting in reliance of any statutory power
A person shall not be liable under subsection (2) where he is acting in the performance of his lawful duties
A person shall not be liable under subsection (2) where he is acting in the discharge of any legal obligation

sections.source_quote: 24. Disclosure of details of an investigation (1) No person shall disclose any detail of a criminal investigation under this Act and the investigation shall be kept confidential.
sections.relevance_reason: Confidentiality of criminal investigations

sections.heading: Obstruction of investigation
sections.title: Obstruction of criminal investigations
sections.summary: This section prohibits actions that obstruct or delay criminal investigations, including destroying, deleting, altering, concealing, modifying, or rendering computer data meaningless. It imposes criminal penalties for such actions, with fines up to one million rupees and penal servitude up to 20 years. Exceptions apply where actions are taken under statutory powers, lawful duties, contractual obligations, or legal obligations.
sections.dtia_summary: Prohibits actions that obstruct investigations, which could impact data preservation and integrity during cross-border investigations.

requirements.text: Any person who destroys, deletes, alters, conceals, modifies or renders computer data meaningless with intent to obstruct or delay an investigation shall commit an offence
On conviction, the contravenor shall be liable to a fine not exceeding one million rupees
On conviction, the contravenor shall be liable to penal servitude for a term not exceeding 20 years
A person shall not be liable under subsection (1) where he is acting in reliance of any statutory power
A person shall not be liable under subsection (1) where he is acting in the performance of his lawful duties
A person shall not be liable under subsection (1) where he is acting in the discharge of any legal obligation

sections.source_quote: 25. Obstruction of investigation (1) Any person who destroys, deletes, alters, conceals, modifies or renders computer data meaningless with intent to obstruct or delay an investigation shall commit an offence and shall, on conviction, be liable to a fine not exceeding one million rupees and to penal servitude for a term not exceeding 20 years.
sections.relevance_reason: Criminal penalties for data obstruction

sections.heading: Expedited preservation and partial disclosure of traffic data
sections.title: Expedited preservation of traffic data for investigations
sections.summary: This section requires that when an investigatory authority has reasonable grounds to believe traffic data is relevant for a criminal investigation and there is a risk of data modification, loss, destruction, or inaccessibility, the person in possession or control of the data must preserve it expeditiously or disclose it to identify service providers and communication paths. Preserved data must be maintained for up to 90 days, with possible extensions if the Court deems it necessary for the investigation.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires expedited preservation of traffic data for investigations, which may impact data retention and handling across borders.

requirements.text: Where an investigatory authority has reasonable grounds to believe that any specified traffic data stored in any computer system or device is reasonably required for a criminal investigation
And there is a risk that the traffic data may be modified, lost, destroyed or rendered inaccessible
The investigatory authority shall serve a notice on the person in possession or control of the traffic data requiring expeditious preservation of such available traffic data
Or disclose required traffic data concerning that communication to identify service providers and the path through which communication was transmitted
The data specified in the notice shall be preserved and its integrity maintained for a period not exceeding 90 days

The period of preservation and maintenance of integrity may be extended for a period exceeding 90 days if the Court is satisfied that an extension is reasonably required for the investigation or prosecution

The period of preservation and maintenance of integrity may be extended for a period exceeding 90 days if the Court is satisfied that there is a real risk that the traffic data may be modified, lost, destroyed or rendered inaccessible

sections.source_quote: 26. Expedited preservation and partial disclosure of traffic data (1) Where an investigatory authority has reasonable grounds to believe that– (a) any specified traffic data stored in any computer system or device, or by means of a computer system, is reasonably required for the purpose of a criminal investigation; and (b) there is a risk that the traffic data may be modified, lost, destroyed or rendered inaccessible, the investigatory authority shall serve a notice on the person who is in possession or control of the traffic data, requiring the person to – (i) undertake expeditious preservation of such available traffic data regardless of whether one or more service providers were involved in the transmission of that communication; or (ii) disclose required traffic data concerning that communication in order to identify the service providers and the path through which communication was transmitted.
sections.relevance_reason: Cross-border data transfer and breach notification

sections.heading: Production order
sections.title: Production orders for criminal investigations
sections.summary: This section allows investigatory authorities to request court-ordered production of data for criminal investigations. It requires that data in computer systems within Mauritius be produced in a form that can be taken away and easily understood, ensuring accessibility for investigations without compromising data integrity.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows court-ordered data production for investigations, which may impact data handling and transfer procedures.

requirements.text: Where the disclosure of data is required for the purpose of a criminal investigation or prosecution of an offence, an investigatory authority may make an application to the Judge in Chambers for an order compelling a person in the territory of Mauritius to submit specified data in that person's possession or control
Which is stored in a computer system or device

An investigatory authority may make an application to the Judge in Chambers for an order compelling any service provider offering its services in the territory of Mauritius to submit subscriber information in relation to such services

Where any material to which an investigation relates consists of computer data stored in a computer system, the request shall be deemed to require the person to produce or give access to it in a form in which it can be taken away and in which it is easily understood

sections.source_quote: 27. Production order (1) Where the disclosure of data is required for the purpose of a criminal investigation or prosecution of an offence, an investigatory authority may make an application to the Judge in Chambers for an order compelling – (a) a person in the territory of Mauritius to submit specified data in that person's possession or control, which is stored in a computer system or device; (b) any service provider offering its services in the territory of Mauritius to submit subscriber information in relation to such services in that service provider's possession or control.
sections.relevance_reason: criminal investigations require data production

sections.heading: Powers of access, search and seizure for purpose of investigation
sections.title: Search and seizure powers for investigations
sections.summary: This section grants investigatory authorities the power to enter premises, access, search, and seize data relevant to criminal investigations. It allows for the seizure of computer systems, retention of copies, maintenance of data integrity, and rendering data inaccessible or removed from systems. It also requires cooperation from individuals with knowledge of computer systems to provide necessary information.
sections.dtia_summary: Provides search and seizure powers for investigations, which may impact data preservation and access during cross-border investigations.

requirements.text: Where an investigatory authority has reasonable grounds to believe that stored data is relevant for the purpose of an investigation or the prosecution of an offence, it may make an application to the Judge in Chambers for a warrant to enter any premises to access, search and seize such data

Where data sought is stored in another computer system or part of it in Mauritian territory, and such data is lawfully accessible from or available to the initial system, the investigatory authority shall expeditiously extend the search or similar access to the other system
The investigatory authority may seize or secure a computer system or part of it or a computer data storage medium
The investigatory authority may make and retain a copy of those computer data
The investigatory authority may maintain the integrity of the relevant stored computer data
The investigatory authority may render inaccessible or remove those computer data from the accessed computer system

The investigatory authority may order any person who has knowledge about the functioning of the computer system or measures applied to protect the computer data therein to provide, as is reasonable, the necessary information

sections.source_quote: 28. Powers of access, search and seizure for purpose of investigation (1) Where an investigatory authority has reasonable grounds to believe that – (a) stored data is relevant for the purpose of an investigation or the prosecution of an offence, it may make an application to the Judge in Chambers for the issue of a warrant to enter any premises to access, search and seize such data; (b) data sought is stored in another computer system or part of it in Mauritian territory, and such data is lawfully accessible from or available to the initial system, the investigatory authority shall expeditiously extend the search or similar access to the other system.
sections.relevance_reason: Search/seizure powers directly impact data access for investigations

sections.heading: Real-time collection of traffic data
sections.title: Real-time collection of traffic data for investigations
sections.summary: This section permits investigatory authorities to request real-time collection of traffic data on Mauritian territory by technical means for criminal investigations. It requires service providers to cooperate in recording communications and maintaining confidentiality regarding the execution of such powers.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows real-time traffic data collection for investigations, which may impact data handling and retention procedures.

requirements.text: Where an investigatory authority has reasonable grounds to believe that traffic data is relevant for the purpose of investigation and prosecution of an offence, it may make an application to the Judge in Chambers for an order authorising the collection or recording of traffic data on Mauritian territory by technical means, in real-time
Associated with specified communications transmitted by means of any computer system
Compelling a service provider, within its technical capabilities, to effect such collection and recording specified in paragraph (a)
Compelling a service provider to keep confidential the fact of the execution of any power provided under this section and any information relating to it

sections.source_quote: 29. Real-time collection of traffic data (1) Where an investigatory authority has reasonable grounds to believe that traffic data is relevant for the purpose of investigation and prosecution of an offence, it may make an application to the Judge in Chambers for an order – (a) authorising the collection or recording of traffic data on Mauritian territory by technical means, in real-time, associated with specified communications transmitted by means of any computer system; (b) compelling a service provider, within its technical capabilities, to – (i) effect such collection and recording specified in paragraph (a); or (ii) cooperate with the investigatory authority to effect such collection and recording; or (c) compelling a service provider to keep confidential the fact of the execution of any power provided under this section and any information relating to it.
sections.relevance_reason: investigatory powers for real-time data collection

sections.heading: Interception of content data
sections.title: Interception of content data by investigatory authorities
sections.summary: Investigatory authorities may apply to a Judge in Chambers for orders to collect or record content data in real-time within Mauritius using technical means. Service providers must cooperate with the authority's collection efforts within their existing technical capabilities. The authority must maintain confidentiality regarding the order's execution and related information.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows real-time data interception within Mauritius for investigations, requiring technical cooperation from service providers and confidentiality of the order's execution. Relevant for data transfer assessments involving law enforcement access.

requirements.text: Collect or record content data in real-time within Mauritius using technical means
Compel service providers to cooperate in real-time data collection within Mauritius
Maintain confidentiality of the order's execution and related information

sections.source_quote: Where an investigatory authority has reasonable grounds to believe that any content data is relevant for the investigation and prosecution of an offence, it may make an application to the Judge in Chambers for an order to – (a) collect or record content data in the territory of Mauritius by technical means in real-time of specified communications by means of a computer system; (b) compel a service provider, within its existing technical capabilities, to – (i) collect or record by technical means on the territory of Mauritius; (ii) cooperate and assist the investigatory authority in the collection or recording of content data, in real-time, of specified communications in the territory of Mauritius, transmitted by means of a computer system; or (c) compel a service provider to keep the confidentiality of the fact of the execution of any power provided for in this section and any information relating to it.
sections.relevance_reason: Cross-border data transfer restrictions

sections.heading: Deletion order
sections.title: Deletion of unlawfully stored computer data
sections.summary: A Judge in Chambers may order the deletion or destruction of computer data if an investigatory authority proves unlawful material or activity exists in a system. The order applies to data no longer being stored or made available through the system.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires deletion of unlawfully stored data to prevent retention of sensitive information, relevant for data storage assessments in law enforcement contexts.

requirements.text: Order computer data to no longer be stored on or made available through the system
Order computer data to be deleted or destroyed

sections.source_quote: The Judge in Chambers may, for the purpose of this Act, upon application by an investigatory authority, and upon being satisfied that a computer system or any other device contains any unlawful material or activity, order that such computer data be – (a) no longer stored on and made available through the computer system or any other device; or (b) deleted or destroyed.
sections.relevance_reason: Deletion of unlawfully stored data directly impacts data retention and compliance

sections.heading: Limited use of disclosed computer data and information
sections.title: Limited use of intercepted computer data
sections.summary: Computer data obtained by authorized persons must be used for criminal investigations or prosecutions unless it relates to other enactments, court orders, public health/safety, or public interest. Authorized persons may refuse access if they have reasonable grounds to believe data possession could lead to criminal activity or prejudice investigations.
sections.dtia_summary: Restricts data usage to specific purposes, relevant for data sharing assessments involving law enforcement data transfers.

requirements.text: Use computer data for criminal investigations or prosecutions
May not use data for purposes other than criminal investigations or prosecutions
May refuse access to data if possession constitutes or may lead to criminal activity
May refuse access to data if providing copies prejudices investigations

sections.source_quote: (1) Computer data obtained under this Act by any person authorised, in writing, by an investigatory authority shall be used for the purpose of a criminal investigation or the prosecution of an offence, unless such computer data is sought in – (a) accordance with any other enactment; (b) compliance with an order from a Court; (c) relation to the prevention of injury or other damage to the health of a person or serious loss of, or damage to, property; or (d) the public interest. (2) Subject to subsections (1) and (3), any person authorised by an investigatory authority shall, on receipt of a request, in writing, permit a person who had the custody or control of a computer system to access and copy computer data on the computer system. (3) Any person authorised, in writing, by an investigatory authority, may refuse to give access to computer data or provide copies of such computer data if he has reasonable grounds to believe that – (a) possession of the data constitutes, or may lead to, or assist in, a criminal offence; or (b) giving access or copies prejudices the investigation in connection with which the search was carried out, another ongoing investigation, or any criminal proceedings that are pending or which may be brought in relation to any of those investigations.
sections.relevance_reason: Limited use of intercepted data for investigations

sections.heading: Critical information infrastructure
sections.title: Critical information infrastructure identification
sections.summary: The Committee identifies critical information infrastructure after consulting regulatory authorities within 3 months. A system is critical if disruption would cause life-sustaining service interruptions, significant economic impacts, massive casualties, or money market failures. Regulatory authorities must monitor and report data classification, security, incident management, and recovery measures.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines critical infrastructure systems requiring specific security measures, relevant for data storage and processing assessments in critical sectors.

requirements.text: Identify information structures needing critical infrastructure declaration within 3 months
System is critical if disruption causes life-sustaining service interruptions
System is critical if disruption causes significant economic impacts
System is critical if disruption causes massive casualties or fatalities
System is critical if disruption causes money market failures
Regulatory authorities must monitor and report data classification
Regulatory authorities must monitor and report data storage and archiving
Regulatory authorities must monitor and report cybersecurity incident management
Regulatory authorities must monitor and report disaster contingency and recovery measures
Regulatory authorities must monitor and report minimum physical and technical security measures
Regulatory authorities must monitor and report cybersecurity incident reporting to CERT-MU
Regulatory authorities must establish a point of contact for critical infrastructure

sections.source_quote: (1) The Committee may, after consultation with the regulatory authority in control of any information infrastructure which is identified as a potential critical information infrastructure, within 3 months of the consultation, identify the information structures which need to be declared critical information infrastructures. (2) A system is selected as a critical information infrastructure if a disruption of the system or its data would result in – (a) the interruption of a life sustaining service such as the supply of water, health services and energy; (b) an important effect on the economy; (c) an event that would result in massive casualties or fatalities; or (d) failure or substantial disruption of the money market. (3) Any regulatory authority shall, within a reasonable time of the declaration of any information infrastructure as a critical information infrastructure, in accordance with the critical information infrastructure framework, monitor and report to the Committee on – (a) the classification of data held by the critical information infrastructure; (b) the protection, storing and archiving of data held by the critical information infrastructure; (c) cybersecurity incident management by the critical information infrastructure; (d) disaster contingency and recovery measures, which may be put place by the critical information infrastructure; (e) minimum physical and technical security measures that may be implemented in order to protect the critical information infrastructure; (f) reporting to CERT-MU cybersecurity incidents impacting national security, public safety and public interest by the owners of critical information infrastructure; (g) the establishment of a point of contact at the level of the critical information infrastructure; (h) any other relevant matter which is necessary or expedient in order to promote cybersecurity in respect of the critical information infrastructure.
sections.relevance_reason: Critical infrastructure definition directly impacts data security and breach response

sections.heading: Protection of critical information infrastructure
sections.title: Critical information infrastructure protection requirements
sections.summary: Regulatory authorities must direct critical infrastructure owners to conduct threat assessments, measure preparedness against cyber-attacks, identify risks, implement security policies, perform periodic risk assessments, establish incident reporting policies, and develop security awareness programs.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires specific security measures for critical infrastructure, relevant for data processing and storage assessments in high-risk sectors.

requirements.text: Conduct threat, vulnerability, risk, and cyber-attack probability assessments
Measure overall preparedness against damage or unauthorized access
Identify other risk factors necessary for critical infrastructure protection
Implement information security policies
Conduct periodic IT security risk assessments
Implement incident reporting policies
Develop security awareness programs

sections.source_quote: Any regulatory authority of the respective critical information infrastructure shall, in consultation with the Committee, direct the owner of a critical information infrastructure to – (a) conduct an assessment of the threats, vulnerabilities, risks and probability of a cyber-attack of the critical information infrastructure; (b) measure the overall preparedness against damage or unauthorised access to a critical information infrastructure; (c) identify any other risk based factors appropriate and necessary to protect the critical information infrastructure; (d) implement information security policy; (e) conduct periodic IT Security Risk Assessment of a critical information infrastructure; (f) implement an incident reporting policy; (g) develop a Security Awareness Programme.
sections.relevance_reason: Critical infrastructure security mandates directly impact data protection and cross-border transfers.

sections.heading: Reports on critical information infrastructure
sections.title: Critical information infrastructure incident reporting
sections.summary: Critical infrastructure owners must report cybersecurity incidents impacting national security, public safety, or public interest to the Committee. The Committee directs CERT-MU to advise mitigation actions, and CERT-MU reports on incidents to the Committee.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires timely incident reporting for critical infrastructure, relevant for breach notification and data security assessments.

requirements.text: Report cybersecurity incidents impacting national security, public safety, or public interest
Report intended mitigation actions for cybersecurity incidents
CERT-MU must advise critical infrastructure owners on incident mitigation
CERT-MU must report cybersecurity incidents to the Committee

sections.source_quote: (1) The owner of a critical information infrastructure shall report to the Committee on any cybersecurity incident impacting the national security, public safety or public interest and the action the owner intends to take to mitigate the cybersecurity incident. (2) The Committee shall, on receipt of a report under subsection (1), direct CERT-MU to advise the owner of a critical information infrastructure on the mitigation of the cybersecurity incident. (3) CERT-MU shall submit a report to the Committee on the reported cybersecurity incidents impacting the national security, public safety and public interest reported by the owners of critical information infrastructure.
sections.relevance_reason: Critical incident reporting for national security/public safety

sections.heading: Information sharing agreement
sections.title: Critical infrastructure data sharing with public entities
sections.summary: Private entities can share critical information infrastructure data with public entities only for enforcing cybersecurity, investigating cybercrimes, or protecting national security, public safety, or public interest as per the critical information infrastructure policy. The sharing must align with the specified purposes to ensure lawful data transfer activities.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires data sharing agreements for critical infrastructure with public entities to be limited to specific security and law enforcement purposes, impacting cross-border data flows for critical systems.

requirements.text: A private entity may enter into an information sharing agreement with a public entity regarding a critical information infrastructure
An agreement shall only be entered into for enforcing cybersecurity
An agreement shall only be entered into for investigating and prosecuting cybercrimes
An agreement shall only be entered into for protecting national security, public safety or public interest

sections.source_quote: 36. Information sharing agreement (1) A private entity may enter into an information sharing agreement with a public entity regarding a critical information infrastructure.
sections.relevance_reason: Critical infrastructure data sharing with public entities for lawful purposes

sections.heading: Auditing of critical information infrastructure to ensure
sections.title: Annual IT security audits for critical infrastructure owners
sections.summary: Critical infrastructure owners must conduct independent IT security audits annually or after major infrastructure changes. Audit reports must be submitted to the National Cybersecurity Committee, and owners must provide additional information if requested to evaluate compliance. Failure to comply results in fines up to 100,000 rupees and imprisonment up to five years.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandates regular security audits for critical infrastructure, requiring transparency with regulators and accountability for non-compliance in cross-border data environments.

requirements.text: The owner of a critical information infrastructure shall carry out an independent IT Security Audit every year or where there is a major upgrade or change in the IT infrastructure
The report of the independent IT Security Audit shall be submitted to the National Cybersecurity Committee to evaluate compliance
The owner shall provide additional information as may be required within a specified period to evaluate any issues raised by the IT Security audit
The owner shall not hinder, obstruct or improperly attempt to influence any person or organisation authorised to carry out the IT Security audit

The owner shall not hinder, obstruct or attempt to influence any member of the Committee, person or entity to monitor, evaluate and report on the adequacy and effectiveness of the findings of the IT Security audit

sections.source_quote: 37. Auditing of critical information infrastructure to ensure compliance (1) The owner of a critical information infrastructure shall, every year or where there is a major upgrade or change in the IT infrastructure, carry out an independent IT Security Audit.
sections.relevance_reason: Mandatory audits for critical infrastructure security

sections.heading: Setting up of CERT-MU
sections.title: Establishment of CERT-MU for cybersecurity coordination
sections.summary: The Ministry establishes CERT-MU as the national agency for coordinating cybersecurity response activities and promoting cybersecurity at the national level. The Director of CERT-MU is appointed and assisted by a team with prescribed qualifications and experience.
sections.dtia_summary: Creates a national cybersecurity agency to coordinate responses and promote best practices, which may influence data handling protocols for critical infrastructure.

requirements.text: There is set up, within the Ministry, for the purposes of this Act, the Computer Emergency Response Team of Mauritius (CERT-MU) which shall be the national agency for coordinating cybersecurity response activities and promoting cybersecurity at national level
The head of CERT-MU shall be the Director of CERT-MU
The Director of CERT-MU shall be assisted by a team of officers with such qualifications and experience as may be prescribed

sections.source_quote: 38. Setting up of CERT-MU (1) There is set up, within the Ministry, for the purposes of this Act, the Computer Emergency Response Team of Mauritius (CERT-MU) which shall be the national agency for coordinating cybersecurity response activities and promoting cybersecurity at national level.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential governance for cybersecurity coordination

sections.heading: Functions of CERT-MU
sections.title: CERT-MU functions for cybersecurity coordination
sections.summary: CERT-MU is tasked with advising the government on cybersecurity policies, coordinating incident responses, providing technical assistance to law enforcement, disseminating alerts, issuing best practice publications, screening network traffic for threats, collecting threat information, promoting awareness, collaborating internationally, and supporting cybersecurity research.
sections.dtia_summary: CERT-MU's role in threat monitoring and incident response affects data handling practices for critical infrastructure and cross-border cybersecurity cooperation.

requirements.text: CERT-MU shall advise and assist the Government on the development and implementation of cybersecurity policies, strategies and best practices
CERT-MU shall coordinate cybersecurity incident response activities
CERT-MU shall provide technical assistance to law enforcement agencies in the resolution of cybersecurity incidents
CERT-MU shall disseminate cybersecurity alerts, advisories, vulnerability notes to organisations and the public
CERT-MU shall issue publications related to cybersecurity best practices
CERT-MU shall screen netflow data for detecting potential cyber threats at the level of Internet Service Providers
CERT-MU shall collect information from organisations for identifying, assessing, monitoring, and responding to cyber threats
CERT-MU shall promote cybersecurity awareness and cyber hygiene at national level
CERT-MU shall collaborate with the international CERT community, industry and regional, sectorial and international security forums for sharing information on cyber threats and related information
CERT-MU shall promote research and development in cybersecurity
CERT-MU shall participate and advise on the Council of Europe's Global Action on Cybercrime Extended project

sections.source_quote: 39. Functions of CERT-MU The functions of CERT-MU shall be to – (a) advise and assist the Government on the development and implementation of cybersecurity policies, strategies and best practices; (b) coordinate cybersecurity incident response activities; (c) provide technical assistance to law enforcement agencies in the resolution of cybersecurity incidents; (d) disseminate cybersecurity alerts, advisories, vulnerability notes to organisations and the public; (e) issue publications related to cybersecurity best practices; (f) screen netflow data for detecting potential cyber threats at the level of Internet Service Providers; (g) collect information from organisations for identifying, assessing, monitoring, and responding to cyber threats; (h) promote cybersecurity awareness and cyber hygiene at national level; (i) collaborate with the international CERT community, industry and regional, sectorial and international security forums for sharing information on cyber threats and related information; (j) promote research and development in cybersecurity; (k) participate and advise on the Council of Europe's Global Action on Cybercrime Extended project.
sections.relevance_reason: Supporting: incident coordination, threat monitoring

sections.heading: General principles relating to international cooperation
sections.title: International cooperation for mutual legal assistance in cyber matters
sections.summary: The Central Authority can request mutual legal assistance from foreign states for cybercrime investigations, evidence collection, data preservation, and disclosure. Requests must comply with confidentiality conditions and specific purposes related to cybercrime, with the Central Authority having the power to refuse requests involving tax offenses.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines conditions for international data transfers in cybercrime investigations, including data preservation and disclosure mechanisms that impact cross-border data flows.

requirements.text: The Central Authority may make a request for mutual legal assistance in any criminal matter to a foreign State for the purpose of undertaking investigations or proceedings concerning offences related to computer systems, electronic communications or computer data
The Central Authority may make a request for mutual legal assistance to a foreign State for collecting evidence of an offence in electronic form
The Central Authority may make a request for mutual legal assistance to a foreign State for collecting evidence in electronic form of any criminal offence

The Central Authority may make a request for mutual legal assistance to a foreign State for obtaining expeditious preservation and disclosure of data, including traffic data, real-time collection of traffic data associated with specified communications or interception of computer data
The Central Authority may require a requesting State to keep the contents, information and materials provided in a confidential manner
The Central Authority may require a requesting State to only use the contents, information and materials provided for the purpose of the criminal matter specified in the request
The Central Authority may require a requesting State to use the contents, information and materials subject to such conditions as may be specified
The Central Authority may request that information be kept confidential or disclosed only subject to such conditions as may be specified

sections.source_quote: 40. General principles relating to international cooperation (2) The Central Authority may make a request for mutual legal assistance in any criminal matter to a foreign State for the purpose of – (a) undertaking investigations or proceedings concerning offences related to computer systems, electronic communications or computer data; (b) collecting evidence of an offence in electronic form; (c) collecting evidence in electronic form of any criminal offence; (d) obtaining expeditious preservation and disclosure of data, including traffic data, real-time collection of traffic data associated with specified communications or interception of computer data or any other means, power, function or provisions under this Act.
sections.relevance_reason: Cross-border data transfer for cybercrime investigations

sections.heading: Spontaneous information
sections.title: Spontaneous sharing of cybercrime investigation information
sections.summary: The Central Authority may share information obtained during Mauritian investigations with foreign states without prior request if it considers the disclosure will assist in initiating or carrying out cybercrime investigations or lead to cooperation requests. The information must be kept confidential or subject to specified conditions, with non-compliance requiring notification to the Central Authority.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows for spontaneous sharing of cybercrime investigation data with foreign states under specific conditions, impacting cross-border data transfers for law enforcement purposes.

requirements.text: The Central Authority may forward to a foreign State information obtained within the framework of a Mauritian investigation where it considers that the disclosure of such information may assist the foreign State in initiating or carrying out investigations or proceedings concerning criminal offences related to cybercrime and cybersecurity
The Central Authority may request that information be kept confidential or disclosed only subject to such conditions as may be specified
A foreign State that does not comply with the conditions specified under subsection (2) shall notify the Central Authority
The Central Authority shall determine whether the foreign State should be provided the information requested for on receipt of a notice under subsection (3)
The Central Authority may refuse to provide the information where the foreign State does not take the commitment to respect the conditions specified by the Central Authority

sections.source_quote: 41. Spontaneous information (1) The Central Authority may, subject to this Act and any other relevant law, without a prior request, forward to a foreign State information obtained within the framework of a Mauritian investigation where it considers that the disclosure of such information may – (a) assist the foreign State in initiating or carrying out investigations or proceedings concerning criminal offences related to cybercrime and cybersecurity; or (b) lead to a request for cooperation by the foreign State under this Act.
sections.relevance_reason: Cross-border cybercrime data sharing with conditions

sections.heading: Expedited preservation of stored computer data
sections.title: Expedited preservation of computer data for mutual legal assistance
sections.summary: A requesting State may request the Central Authority to preserve computer data within Mauritius for mutual legal assistance purposes. The request must specify the authority seeking preservation, the related criminal investigation, the data to be preserved, its connection to the offence, custodian information, necessity of preservation, and intent to submit a mutual assistance request. The Central Authority must preserve the data for up to 120 days to enable the requesting State's subsequent actions.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires specific conditions for expeditious preservation of computer data across borders for mutual legal assistance, with a 120-day limit on preservation period. This affects data retention and cross-border data handling in criminal investigations.

requirements.text: A requesting State may request the Central Authority to obtain expeditious preservation of stored computer data within Mauritius
The request must specify the authority seeking preservation
The request must specify the offence and brief summary of related facts
The request must specify the stored computer data and its connection to the offence
The request must specify any available information identifying the custodian or location of the computer system
The request must specify the necessity of preservation
The request must specify the intention to submit a mutual assistance request
The Central Authority must preserve the data in accordance with procedures and powers under this Act
The purpose of preservation must be to enable the State to submit a mutual assistance request
The data must be preserved for a period not exceeding 120 days
The data must continue to be preserved pending final decision on the request

sections.source_quote: Expedited preservation of stored computer data (1) A requesting State which intends to make a request for mutual legal assistance for the search or similar access, seizure or similar securing or the disclosure of computer data, may request the Central Authority to obtain the expeditious preservation of stored computer data located within the territory of Mauritius.
sections.relevance_reason: Cross-border mutual legal assistance data preservation

sections.heading: Expedited disclosure of preserved traffic data
sections.title: Expedited disclosure of traffic data to identify foreign service providers
sections.summary: If an investigating agency discovers a service provider in another State transmitted communications, the Central Authority must expeditiously disclose traffic data to identify that service provider and the communication path. Disclosure may only be withheld if it prejudices Mauritius' sovereignty, security, public order, or public interest.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires disclosure of traffic data to identify foreign service providers for cross-border investigations, with a condition that disclosure may be withheld if it threatens national security or public interest.

requirements.text: Where an investigating agency discovers a service provider in another State transmitted communications, the Central Authority shall expeditiously disclose sufficient traffic data to identify that service provider and the communication path
Disclosure may only be withheld if the Central Authority considers execution of the request likely to prejudice Mauritius' sovereignty, security, public order or public interest

sections.source_quote: Expedited disclosure of preserved traffic data (1) Where, in the course of executing a request under section 41 with respect to a specified communication, the investigating agency discovers that a service provider in another State was involved in the transmission of the communication, the Central Authority shall expeditiously disclose to the requesting State a sufficient amount of traffic data to identify that service provider and the path through which the communication was transmitted.
sections.relevance_reason: Cross-border data transfer authority

sections.heading: Mutual assistance regarding accessing of stored computer data
sections.title: Mutual assistance for accessing stored computer data in criminal investigations
sections.summary: A requesting State may request the Central Authority to search, seize, or disclose computer data within Mauritius for criminal investigations. The request must specify the investigating authority, nature of criminal matter, purpose of request, and details of data to be accessed. The Central Authority must obtain necessary authorisation, including warrants, to execute the request and may seek cooperation from the requesting State.
sections.dtia_summary: Provides framework for cross-border access to computer data during criminal investigations, requiring specific information and authorisation, with potential for expedited processing if data is vulnerable to loss or modification.

requirements.text: A requesting State may request the Commissioner of Police through the Central Authority to search or similarly access, seize or similarly secure, and disclose stored computer data within Mauritius
The requesting State must provide the name of the authority conducting the investigation
The requesting State must provide a description of the nature of the criminal matter and summary of relevant facts and laws
The requesting State must provide a description of the purpose of the request and nature of assistance sought
The requesting State must provide details of the period within which the request should be complied with
The Central Authority must obtain necessary authorisation, including any warrants, to execute the request
The Central Authority may seek support and cooperation of the requesting State during search and seizure
The Central Authority must provide results of search and details of electronic or physical evidence seized
The request must be responded to on an expedited basis if relevant data is particularly vulnerable to loss or modification

sections.source_quote: Mutual assistance regarding accessing of stored computer data (1) A requesting State may request the Commissioner of Police, through the Central Authority, to search or similarly access, seize or similarly secure, and disclose stored computer data located within the territory of Mauritius, including computer data that are specified in section 40.
sections.relevance_reason: Requires warrants for data access in criminal investigations

sections.heading: Transborder access to stored computer data with consent or
sections.title: Cross-border access to computer data with consent or public availability
sections.summary: Investigatory authorities may access publicly available computer data regardless of geographic location. They may also access computer data in another State if a police officer or authorised person obtains lawful and voluntary consent from the person with authority to disclose the data through a Mauritius computer system.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows cross-border access to computer data under two conditions: public availability or with explicit consent from the data controller, without requiring additional authorisation from another State.

requirements.text: Investigatory authority may access publicly available stored computer data regardless of geographic location
