Investigatory authority may access or receive stored computer data in another State if a police officer or authorised person obtains lawful and voluntary consent from the person with authority to disclose the data

sections.source_quote: Transborder access to stored computer data with consent or where publicly available The investigatory authority may, without the authorisation of another State, and subject to this Act – (a) access publicly available stored computer data, regardless of where the data is located geographically; or (b) access or receive, through a computer system in Mauritius, stored computer data located in another State, if a police officer or authorised person obtains the lawful and voluntary consent of the person who has the lawful authority to disclose the data to Mauritius through that computer system.
sections.relevance_reason: Cross-border access with consent

sections.heading: Mutual assistance in real-time collection of traffic data
sections.title: Real-time collection of traffic data for cross-border investigations
sections.summary: A requesting State may request the Central Authority to provide real-time collection of traffic data associated with communications in Mauritius. The request must specify the authority, offence details, data location, purpose, and terms for data use. The Central Authority must obtain necessary authorisation, including warrants, and may seek cooperation from the requesting State.
sections.dtia_summary: Enables real-time cross-border traffic data collection for investigations, requiring specific information and authorisation, with potential for expedited processing.

requirements.text: A requesting State may request the Central Authority to provide assistance in real-time collection of traffic data associated with specified communications in Mauritius
The requesting State must specify the authority seeking use of powers
The requesting State must specify the offence and brief summary of related facts
The requesting State must specify the name of the authority with access to relevant traffic data
The requesting State must specify the location where traffic data may be held
The requesting State must specify the intended purpose of requiring traffic data
The Central Authority must obtain necessary authorisation, including any warrant to execute the request
The Central Authority may seek support and cooperation of the requesting State during collection

sections.source_quote: Mutual assistance in real-time collection of traffic data (1) A requesting State may request the Central Authority to provide assistance in real-time collection of traffic data associated with specified communications in Mauritius, transmitted by means of a computer system.
sections.relevance_reason: Cross-border data transfer authority

sections.heading: Mutual assistance regarding interception of content data
sections.title: Real-time interception of content data for cross-border investigations
sections.summary: A requesting State may request the Central Authority to provide real-time interception or recording of content data for specified communications in Mauritius. The request must specify the authority, offence details, intended duration, and terms for data use. The Central Authority must obtain necessary authorisation, including warrants, and may seek cooperation from the requesting State.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows real-time interception of content data across borders for investigations, requiring specific information and authorisation, with potential for expedited processing.

requirements.text: A requesting State may request the Central Authority to provide assistance in real-time collection or recording of content data of specified communications in Mauritius
The requesting State must specify the authority seeking use of powers
The requesting State must specify the offence and brief summary of related facts
The requesting State must specify the name of the authority with access to relevant communication
The requesting State must specify the intended duration of interception
The requesting State must specify the reason for using powers
The requesting State must specify terms and conditions for use and disclosure of communication to third parties
The Central Authority must obtain necessary authorisation, including any warrant to execute the request
The Central Authority may seek support and cooperation of the requesting State during interception

sections.source_quote: Mutual assistance regarding interception of content data (1) A requesting State may request the Central Authority to provide assistance in the real-time collection or recording of content data of specified communications in the territory of Mauritius transmitted by means of a computer system.
sections.relevance_reason: Cross-border data interception for investigations

sections.heading: 24/7 point of contact
sections.title: 24/7 point of contact for cross-border cybercrime assistance
sections.summary: This section mandates the Commissioner of Police to establish a 24/7 point of contact for immediate assistance in cross-border cybercrime investigations. The point of contact must provide technical advice, preserve data, collect evidence, and locate suspects within defined timelines. It also requires facilitating international extradition and mutual assistance under the Budapest Convention.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes a mechanism for urgent cross-border cybercrime cooperation, requiring data preservation and evidence collection that could impact data transfer impact assessments for international investigations.

requirements.text: The Commissioner of Police shall designate a 24/7 point of contact available on twenty-four-hour, seven-day-a-week basis
Provide technical advice
Preserve data pursuant to sections 42 and 43
Collect evidence within expeditious timelines defined by regulations
Acquire trained and equipped personnel to facilitate the operation of this Act
Facilitate or directly carry out technical advice, data preservation, evidence collection, legal information, and suspect location
Provide technical advice for stopping or tracing cyberattacks
Facilitate international extradition or mutual assistance
Carry out communications with other parties to the Budapest Convention on an expedited basis

sections.source_quote: 48. 24/7 point of contact (1) The Commissioner of Police shall designate a 24/7 point of contact available on twenty-four-hour, seven-day-a-week basis in order to provide immediate assistance to the point of contact of another Party on an expedited basis, for the purpose of investigations or proceedings concerning criminal offences related to computer systems and computer data, or for the collection of evidence in electronic form of a criminal offence.
sections.relevance_reason: Cross-border cybercrime assistance directly impacts data transfers and international data handling.

sections.heading: Jurisdiction
sections.title: Jurisdiction for cybercrime offences
sections.summary: This section grants the Intermediate Court exclusive jurisdiction over cybercrime offences under this Act, including cases committed outside Mauritius on Mauritian ships or aircraft. It allows the court to impose penalties and forfeitures as provided by the Act.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes jurisdictional scope for cybercrime cases, potentially affecting data transfer impact assessments when cross-border cybercrime investigations involve Mauritian jurisdiction.

requirements.text: The Intermediate Court shall have jurisdiction to try any offence under this Act or any regulations made under it
The Intermediate Court may impose any penalty or forfeiture provided under this Act
The Intermediate Court has jurisdiction where an offence under this Act is committed outside Mauritius on board a Mauritian ship
The Intermediate Court has jurisdiction where an offence under this Act is committed outside Mauritius on board an aircraft registered in Mauritius

sections.source_quote: 50. Jurisdiction (1) Notwithstanding any other enactment, the Intermediate Court shall have jurisdiction to try any offence under this Act or any regulations made under it and may, on conviction, impose any penalty or forfeiture provided under this Act.
sections.relevance_reason: Jurisdiction for cybercrime offences

sections.heading: Forfeiture
sections.title: Forfeiture of cybercrime equipment
sections.summary: This section allows courts to order the forfeiture of any apparatus, article, or device used in connection with cybercrime offences, in addition to penalties imposed on convicted individuals. It applies to equipment directly related to the offence.
sections.dtia_summary: This section may impact data transfer impact assessments by requiring the seizure of equipment used in cross-border cybercrime investigations.

requirements.text: The Court before which a person is convicted of an offence may order the forfeiture of any apparatus, article or device which is the subject matter of the offence or is used in connection with the commission of the offence

sections.source_quote: 53. Forfeiture The Court before which a person is convicted of an offence may, in addition to any other penalty imposed, order the forfeiture of any apparatus, article or device which is the subject matter of the offence or is used in connection with the commission of the offence.
sections.relevance_reason: Forfeiture of cybercrime equipment unrelated to data compliance

sections.heading: Repeal
sections.title: Repeal of previous cybercrime law
sections.summary: This section explicitly repeals the Computer Misuse and Cybercrime Act, transferring all relevant orders and pending applications to the current Act upon its commencement.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is irrelevant to data transfer impact assessments as it deals with the repeal of a previous law without operational impact on current data transfers.
sections.source_quote: 54. Repeal The Computer Misuse and Cybercrime Act is repealed.
sections.relevance_reason: Repeals previous law, no direct data compliance impact

sections.heading: Savings and transitional provisions
sections.title: Savings and transitional provisions for repealed law
sections.summary: This section ensures that orders and pending applications made under the repealed Computer Misuse and Cybercrime Act remain valid under the current Act upon its commencement. It also allows the Minister to make regulations for any transitions not covered by this section.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is irrelevant to data transfer impact assessments as it addresses the transition of repealed legal mechanisms without affecting current data transfer practices.
sections.source_quote: 55. Savings and transitional provisions (1) Any order made under the repealed Computer Misuse and Cybercrime Act and which is valid before the commencement of this Act shall, on the commencement of this Act, be deemed to have been made under this Act and shall be dealt with in accordance with the relevant provisions of this Act.
sections.relevance_reason: repealed law transition mechanics
